name: Fête du Citron (Menton Lemon Festival)
tagline: A Riviera border town builds castles, dragons and whole fantasy worlds out of 140 tonnes of lemons and oranges while the rest of Europe shivers.
dates: 13 February – 1 March 2027 (expected; 2026 edition ran 14 Feb – 1 Mar, aligned with Nice Carnival)
history: Menton's winter festivities date back to 1875, when local hoteliers dreamed up a carnival to entertain the aristocrats and consumptives wintering on the mildest stretch of the Riviera — Queen Victoria herself attended the 1882 edition, which closed with fireworks over Garavan Bay. The citrus angle arrived in 1928, when Menton was still the largest lemon producer on the European continent and a hotelier staged a private exhibition of fruit and flowers in the gardens of the Riviera Hotel. The town hall took the idea over the following year, moved it into the public gardens, and in 1934 gave the event its now-trademarked name: the Fête du Citron.

The format that survives today crystallised around two set pieces: the corsos — golden daytime parades (and, on certain evenings, illuminated night parades) of floats armoured in hundreds of thousands of individually wired lemons and oranges — and the monumental citrus sculptures displayed for the full fortnight in the Jardins Biovès. Since 1959 each edition has taken a theme, from Tintin and Asterix to Bollywood, opera and rock, and the builders now use roughly 140 tonnes of fruit, much of it trucked in from Spain since Menton's own prized IGP-labelled lemons are too valuable (and too few) to staple to chicken wire. In a very French touch, the fruit is sold off cheaply to the public once the festival ends.

Suspended only for the Second World War, the 1991 Gulf War and the 2021 pandemic year, the festival was inscribed in France's inventory of intangible cultural heritage in 2019 and now draws well over 200,000 visitors — the second-biggest winter event on the Côte d'Azur after the Nice Carnival, with which it deliberately shares its dates. That pairing is the traveller's cheat code: base yourself between the two and you can watch flower battles in Nice by day and torch-lit citrus floats in Menton by night in the same week.
whyGo: Nowhere else on earth turns citrus into monumental public art at this scale — ten-metre dragons, temples and storybook scenes built lemon by lemon, glowing gold against belle-époque façades and a February sea. It clusters perfectly with Nice Carnival for a double-header Riviera week, and it happens in the one corner of Europe where mid-winter genuinely feels like spring.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Nice Côte d'Azur (NCE) with SWISS or easyJet, then tram/bus to Nice-Ville and TER train Nice → Menton (35–40 min)
gettingThere.notes: Multiple daily nonstops, ~1h20 in the air; TER trains run every 30 min along the coast — sit on the right (sea) side

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Milano Centrale (EC direct, 3h15) → Ventimiglia (Intercity, ~4h) → Menton (TER, 10 min)
gettingThere.notes: Book the Zürich–Milan EC as an SBB supersaver up to 3 months out; the Ligurian coastal stretch after Genoa is spectacular. An overnight in Milan splits it nicely

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Gotthard tunnel → Milan → A10/A8 autostrada → Ventimiglia → Menton
gettingThere.cost: ≈€120–180 in fuel + Swiss vignette and Italian/French tolls
gettingThere.notes: Parking in Menton during the festival is miserable — park at a garage on the edge of town or stay in Roquebrune/Ventimiglia and train in

costs.notes: Estimates for flights/rail from Zürich, 2–3 nights and event tickets. Riviera hotels spike hard during festival + Nice Carnival weeks — Menton itself sells out first; staying in Ventimiglia (Italy, 10 min by train) roughly halves the room rate. Event tickets are modest: gardens entry ~€16, standing parade ~€17, grandstand seat ~€27–32 (expected, based on 2026 pricing).

booking.label: Official site & tickets — Fête du Citron

weather: Menton is famously the warmest spot in mainland France: expect 8–15°C, plenty of sun, occasional rain and chilly evenings, especially at the night parades. Pack layers, sunglasses and a warm jacket for after dark; a light rain shell covers the rest.
tips: Buy parade and Jardins Biovès tickets online well in advance — grandstand seats for the Sunday golden corsos sell out weeks ahead, and entry gates close 15 minutes after each parade starts.
Visit the Biovès gardens sculptures twice: once in daylight for detail, once during the 'Jardins de Lumières' evening openings when everything is illuminated.
Come on a weekday if you can — Sundays the tourist office itself warns you to arrive before 11:00, and the town of 28,000 absorbs tens of thousands of day-trippers.

Pair it with Nice Carnival: stay 3 nights, do a Nice flower battle one day and Menton's corso the next — the 35-minute TER train makes both trivially easy, but buy train tickets from the machine early as queues balloon on parade days.
Sleep in Ventimiglia or Roquebrune-Cap-Martin to dodge festival hotel prices; both are under 15 minutes from Menton by train.

Buy real Menton IGP lemons, limoncello and lemon jam from producers like Maison Herbin rather than the parade fruit — the sculptures mostly use Spanish citrus, and the local lemon is the genuinely rare delicacy.
Leave pets at home for parade days — they're banned from the parade routes and the Palais de l'Europe.

localRecs.name: Mirazur
localRecs.note: Mauro Colagreco's three-Michelin-star temple above the Italian border, once ranked world's No. 1 — its own gardens grow Menton lemons; book months ahead for a once-in-a-trip splurge

localRecs.name: Vieille Ville & Basilique Saint-Michel
localRecs.note: Menton's ochre-and-pink old town stacks up the hillside to a baroque basilica; climb it at golden hour for the classic view over the campanile and the sea

localRecs.name: Jardin Serre de la Madone
localRecs.note: Lawrence Johnston's dreamlike 1920s terraced garden in the Gorbio valley — the horticultural side of the microclimate that made Menton lemon country

localRecs.name: Maison Herbin
localRecs.note: Artisan confiturier in the old town making jams and candied fruit from genuine IGP Menton lemons — the edible souvenir worth carrying home

localRecs.name: Marché des Halles
localRecs.note: The 1898 covered market near the seafront: socca, barbajuans, local citrus and cheap excellent lunches away from parade-route pricing
